efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Here's a brilliant trick you'll use again and again! Put **"hot"** in a sentence, then swap it for **"cold"** — if the meaning **completely flips**, you've found the antonym. It's like pressing a reve
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot simple opposite pairs quickly — you'll be a pro at this!
How to use the flip test — if the meaning reverses completely, it's an antonym (a word that means the opposite). This trick works every time!

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Think about **"expand"** — it means to grow larger or spread out. Now imagine you're watching a video of that action and you press REWIND. What does the reversed action look like? That's your antonym!
lessonLearningGoal: How to find opposites for action words (verbs) — imagine pressing rewind!
Why picturing the REVERSE action helps you find the match every time

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **"rough"** describes the **texture** of a surface. An adjective antonym must describe the **same quality** but in the **opposite direction**.
lessonLearningGoal: How to find opposites for describing words (adjectives) — think about what quality is being described
Why checking WHAT is being described helps you pick the right antonym every time

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: 'Hot' and 'cold' are GRADED antonyms — there are steps in between (warm, cool). You can be a bit hot or very hot.
lessonLearningGoal: How to tell the difference between graded antonyms (opposites with levels in between, like hot-warm-cool-cold) and complementary antonyms (opposites with nothing in between, like alive/dead) — this is fascinating stuff!
Why graded antonyms have 'in-between' steps but complementary ones don't — once you see it, you can't unsee it

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **"rough"** describes the **texture** of a surface. An adjective antonym must describe the **same quality** but in the **opposite direction**.
lessonLearningGoal: How to find opposites for describing words (adjectives) — think about what quality is being described
Why checking WHAT is being described helps you pick the right antonym every time

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **"brave"** means having courage and not showing fear. The opposite emotion must be about the **same thing** (e.g. courage, patience, happiness) and at the **same intensity** (medium-strong).
lessonLearningGoal: How to find opposite emotions at the same intensity level — like turning a dial, not smashing a button!
Why matching the intensity matters for emotion antonyms

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **"benevolent"** means well-meaning and kind. Even when the words look scary, you have two brilliant strategies: **word roots** (prefixes/suffixes that give clues) and **elimination** (crossing out wh
lessonLearningGoal: How to recognise advanced antonym pairs that appear in the 11+ — these are the ones that separate good scores from great ones!
How to use elimination and word roots to work out unfamiliar pairs (you'll feel like a word detective)

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.subConceptName: Prefix Antonyms (un-, dis-, im-, in-, ir-)
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: The prefix **"un-"** means "not" or "the opposite of". It flips the meaning!
lessonLearningGoal: How to use prefixes like un-, dis-, im-, in- or ir- to create an opposite — it's like having a magic switch!
How to choose which prefix goes with which word (there's a pattern, and once you spot it, you'll never forget)

efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **"rough"** describes the **texture** of a surface. An adjective antonym must describe the **same quality** but in the **opposite direction**.
lessonLearningGoal: How to find opposites for describing words (adjectives) — think about what quality is being described
Why checking WHAT is being described helps you pick the right antonym every time

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.

lessonLearningGoal: How to find opposites for action words (verbs) — imagine pressing rewind!

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.subConceptName: Prefix Antonyms (un-, dis-, im-, in-, ir-)
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: The prefix **"un-"** means "not" or "the opposite of". It flips the meaning!
lessonLearningGoal: How to use prefixes like un-, dis-, im-, in- or ir- to create an opposite — it's like having a magic switch!
How to choose which prefix goes with which word (there's a pattern, and once you spot it, you'll never forget)

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **"brave"** means having courage and not showing fear. The opposite emotion must be about the **same thing** (e.g. courage, patience, happiness) and at the **same intensity** (medium-strong).
lessonLearningGoal: How to find opposite emotions at the same intensity level — like turning a dial, not smashing a button!
Why matching the intensity matters for emotion antonyms

efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **"benevolent"** means well-meaning and kind. Even when the words look scary, you have two brilliant strategies: **word roots** (prefixes/suffixes that give clues) and **elimination** (crossing out wh
lessonLearningGoal: How to recognise advanced antonym pairs that appear in the 11+ — these are the ones that separate good scores from great ones!

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Here's a brilliant trick you'll use again and again! Put **"hot"** in a sentence, then swap it for **"cold"** — if the meaning **completely flips**, you've found the antonym. It's like pressing a reve
lessonLearningGoal: How to spot simple opposite pairs quickly — you'll be a pro at this!
How to use the flip test — if the meaning reverses completely, it's an antonym (a word that means the opposite). This trick works every time!

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: 'Hot' and 'cold' are GRADED antonyms — there are steps in between (warm, cool). You can be a bit hot or very hot.
lessonLearningGoal: How to tell the difference between graded antonyms (opposites with levels in between, like hot-warm-cool-cold) and complementary antonyms (opposites with nothing in between, like alive/dead) — this is fascinating stuff!
Why graded antonyms have 'in-between' steps but complementary ones don't — once you see it, you can't unsee it

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: Think about **"expand"** — it means to grow larger or spread out. Now imagine you're watching a video of that action and you press REWIND. What does the reversed action look like? That's your antonym!
lessonLearningGoal: How to find opposites for action words (verbs) — imagine pressing rewind!
Why picturing the REVERSE action helps you find the match every time

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: **"rough"** describes the **texture** of a surface. An adjective antonym must describe the **same quality** but in the **opposite direction**.
lessonLearningGoal: How to find opposites for describing words (adjectives) — think about what quality is being described
Why checking WHAT is being described helps you pick the right antonym every time

efficacyPairs.questionText: Choose one word from each group that are most opposite in meaning.
efficacyPairs.lessonTeachSummary: 'Hot' and 'cold' are GRADED antonyms — there are steps in between (warm, cool). You can be a bit hot or very hot.
lessonLearningGoal: How to tell the difference between graded antonyms (opposites with levels in between, like hot-warm-cool-cold) and complementary antonyms (opposites with nothing in between, like alive/dead) — this is fascinating stuff!
Why graded antonyms have 'in-between' steps but complementary ones don't — once you see it, you can't unsee it

prefix-antonyms.name: Prefix Antonyms (un-, dis-, im-, in-, ir-)
